does: This paper introduces AUVIC, an adversarial unlearning framework designed to remove specific visual concepts from multimodal large language models without full retraining. The core method applies adversarial perturbations to the model to enable precise forgetting of target visual concepts while preserving performance on related entities. The authors construct VCUBench, the first benchmark for evaluating visual concept unlearning in group contexts, and demonstrate through experiments that AUVIC achieves state-of-the-art performance in selectively removing target concepts from MLLMs while maintaining model functionality on similar visual categories and general downstream tasks.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning, which addresses regulatory requirements like the right to be forgotten by enabling selective removal of learned information without costly retraining. While unlearning has been extensively studied for text-based models, visual concept unlearning in multimodal systems remains largely unexplored. The paper combines insights from adversarial training and unlearning literature, extending concepts from text-domain machine unlearning to the vision-language setting. It responds to growing concerns about privacy and intellectual property in large-scale datasets used to train MLLMs, and builds on prior work in model editing and concept removal by introducing an adversarial perturbation approach tailored to the multimodal context.
enables: This work enables practical implementation of data privacy and copyright protection in deployed MLLMs without expensive retraining, supporting compliance with regulations requiring the right to be forgotten. It establishes VCUBench as a standardized evaluation framework for future visual unlearning research. The adversarial perturbation approach opens pathways for more efficient concept removal methods and could inspire similar techniques for other sensitive content removal scenarios in multimodal models. Future work may extend this framework to handle multiple concepts simultaneously, improve efficiency further, or develop theoretical guarantees about unlearning precision and collateral effects on related concepts.
